old_claim.note: Orbital Habitat Cities is a plausible dependency for a forecast technology and should be treated as speculative.

new_claim.note: Closed-loop life support supplies the air, water, recycling, food, and lighting systems required for sustained habitation inside mined asteroid caverns.

source_shape.source_locator: The introduction says sustaining human life on a station inside a mined asteroid requires air, water, recycling systems, food, light, and sufficient gravity.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Human habitation inside a mined asteroid directly requires life-support systems rather than a prior orbital habitat city.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old edge made a large orbital city a prerequisite; the source supports the underlying life-support capability needed by the smaller asteroid-cavern habitat.

ontology_before: The graph made asteroid habitat caverns depend on orbital habitat cities, implying a large orbital settlement must precede or enable the asteroid-cavern concept.
ontology_after: The graph makes asteroid habitat caverns depend on closed-loop life support directly while leaving orbital habitat cities as a separate, broader settlement form.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: asteroid_habitat_caverns now depends on closed_loop_life_support and no longer directly on orbital_habitat_cities.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to asteroid neighborhoods within mature orbital habitat city systems rather than standalone mined-asteroid habitats.
A source shows orbital habitat cities are prerequisite to asteroid habitat caverns rather than an adjacent settlement architecture.
A dependent node requires large civic orbital settlements and should point to orbital_habitat_cities separately.
short_reason: Closed-loop life support is the direct habitation prerequisite; orbital habitat cities were too broad and adjacent.
